name: Yamata no Orochi
description: Yamata no Orochi, meaning "eight-forked serpent" or "eight-headed serpent," is a colossal serpentine dragon from Japanese mythology. This monstrous creature is described as having eight heads and eight tails, each possessing its own unique and terrifying characteristics. Its eyes are said to be as red as winter cherries, and its belly is constantly inflamed and covered in blood. The dragon's immense size allows it to span eight valleys and eight hills, causing widespread destruction and terror. It is associated with storms, floods, and general chaos, representing a powerful force of nature that must be overcome. Its breath is poisonous, capable of withering vegetation and contaminating water sources. The slaying of Yamata no Orochi by the hero Susanoo-no-Mikoto is a pivotal event in Japanese mythology, signifying the triumph of order over chaos and the establishment of civilization.
subtitle: Eight-headed dragon of Japanese myth
appearance: A gigantic serpent with eight heads and eight tails. Its eyes are red like winter cherries, and its belly is perpetually covered in blood and inflammation. Scales are dark and shimmering.
habitat: Ancient Japan, specifically the region of Izumo. Said to dwell in caves and bodies of water.
behavior: Rampant and destructive, driven by insatiable hunger and a desire for chaos. It demands virgin sacrifices.

keyMyths.title: Susanoo's Triumph
keyMyths.description: Susanoo-no-Mikoto, banished from the heavens, encounters an elderly couple weeping over the impending sacrifice of their daughter, Kushinada-hime, to Yamata no Orochi. Susanoo devises a plan, instructing them to brew eight vats of strong sake. The dragon, lured by the aroma, consumes the sake and becomes heavily intoxicated. Susanoo then slays the dragon, severing each head and tail.

keyMyths.title: The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
keyMyths.description: While slaying Yamata no Orochi, Susanoo discovers the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Grass-Cutting Sword) within one of the dragon's tails. This legendary sword becomes one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing the emperor's power and legitimacy. The sword's discovery further emphasizes the significance of Susanoo's victory and its lasting impact on Japanese history and mythology.

keyMyths.title: Symbolism of the Dragon
keyMyths.description: Yamata no Orochi is often interpreted as a representation of natural disasters, such as floods and famines, that plagued ancient Japan. Its defeat by Susanoo symbolizes the efforts of early rulers to control these forces and establish a stable society. The eight heads and tails can also be seen as representing the eight provinces of the Izumo region, which Susanoo brought under his control.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle of Japan. Provides the primary account of Susanoo's encounter with and slaying of Yamata no Orochi.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle of Japan. Provides the primary account of Susanoo's encounter with and slaying of Yamata no Orochi.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another ancient chronicle of Japan. Offers an alternative, though similar, account of the Yamata no Orochi myth.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another ancient chronicle of Japan. Offers an alternative, though similar, account of the Yamata no Orochi myth.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.work: The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan
sources.description: Provides an anthropological perspective on Japanese mythology, including interpretations of the Yamata no Orochi myth within the context of shamanistic beliefs and practices.
sources.text: Provides an anthropological perspective on Japanese mythology, including interpretations of the Yamata no Orochi myth within the context of shamanistic beliefs and practices.

sources.author: Miyata, Noboru
sources.work: The Origin of the Orochi Myth
sources.description: An analysis of the possible origins of the myth of Yamata no Orochi and it's link to iron production.
sources.text: An analysis of the possible origins of the myth of Yamata no Orochi and it's link to iron production.
